feedback.text: Regarding the wording in the mission letter by Michael McGrath from September, 2024 regarding "gambling-like features in video games." We must clearly define what's a "gambling-like feature." I propose the following definition: "A gambling-like feature is part of a game where the random factor directly or indirectly interacts with money" Examples: A random item is generated only if paid by a virtual currency that can also be bought with money *is* a gambling-like feature. (gacha mechanics) A random item is generated and can then be sold at an in-game market and somehow turned into a virtual currency that could also be bought with money *is* a gambling-like feature (in-game premium currency). There are more examples, but I tried to make clear that the unifying factor here is the relation between randomness to money. Why do I think this is important? Because the discussion about what is and isn't a gambling-like feature is easily derailed by bad-faith arguments that try to equate gambling with randomness in general, for example. PS: I support Stop Destroying Videogames.
